medications.indication_rationale: Symptomatic relief only — lubricant drops soothe the gritty foreign-body sensation and dilute inflammatory mediators. Viral conjunctivitis is self-limiting so treatment is comfort-directed. The source note advised preservative-free drops regularly; preservative-free is preferred with frequent dosing.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1-2 drops into each eye up to every 2 hours as needed for comfort

medications.indication_rationale: Antibiotics do not treat a viral conjunctivitis and are not indicated for this watery, follicular, post-URI presentation. Routinely prescribing them for 'pink eye' is an error of commission — they add cost/side-effects and reinforce misuse. They would only be reconsidered if the discharge became frankly purulent, suggesting bacterial infection.

Topical steroids must NOT be started in primary care / telehealth for an undifferentiated red eye — they can cause an unrecognised herpes simplex dendritic keratitis to progress to a sight-threatening geographic ulcer, and can raise intraocular pressure. This is a dangerous error of commission; steroids are an ophthalmologist-only decision after slit-lamp exclusion of HSV.
Antihistamines target allergic conjunctivitis (itch-dominant). This presentation is infective (viral) with grittiness rather than itch and a viral prodrome, so an antihistamine is not indicated.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply cool compresses to the closed eyes several times a day (the source advised about four times daily) and gently clean crusting from the lid margins with cooled boiled water on a fresh piece of cotton for each eye.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cool compresses and lid hygiene relieve the grittiness, swelling and crusting without any active drug while the infection self-resolves.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strict hand hygiene and single-use tissues: wash hands frequently, use a fresh disposable tissue for each dab of the eye and bin it immediately, and do NOT use a reusable cloth handkerchief (which re-seeds the virus).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Viral (adenoviral) conjunctivitis is highly contagious for up to ~12 days; scrupulous hand and tissue hygiene is the main way to prevent auto-reinfection and spread.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not share towels, pillowcases, face cloths or eye make-up; wash towels and pillowcases in hot water; stay away from close work contacts until the eyes are settling, and do not wear contact lenses until the eyes have been white and symptom-free for 48 hours (discard the current lenses/case).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Fomite spread through shared linen and the workplace is the main transmission route; contact-lens avoidance prevents both re-inoculation and lens-related keratitis.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a viral eye infection — essentially the same virus family as the recent cold, now affecting the surface of the eyes. Antibiotic drops will not help because it is not a bacterial infection; the goal is to keep the eyes comfortable and clean while your immune system clears it.

It is self-limiting but highly contagious: symptoms often peak around days 3-5 and can take up to 2-3 weeks to fully settle, and the second eye commonly becomes involved shortly after the first. It can be passed on for up to about 12 days, so hygiene matters.
Do not wear contact lenses until your eyes have been perfectly white and comfortable for 48 hours, wash pillowcases and towels in hot water, and avoid sharing any personal items or eye make-up.

Seek prompt review rather than continuing to treat at home if you develop severe or deep eye pain, sensitivity to light, haloes around lights, any lasting drop in vision that does not clear when you wipe your eyes, or if the discharge turns thick and yellow-green.

follow_up_plan.interval: Self-care now; see your regular GP (or telehealth GP) in about 3-5 days if symptoms are not improving, or sooner if they worsen or the discharge becomes purulent
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Whether symptoms are settling as expected, development of any sight-threatening red-flag features, and whether the discharge has changed to a purulent (possible bacterial) picture.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Severe/deep eye pain, photophobia, haloes, persistent visual loss, a thick yellow-green purulent discharge, or a contact-lens-related red painful eye.

must_include_items: Recognise a benign, self-limiting infective (viral) conjunctivitis pattern from the history
Actively screen the sight-threatening red-eye red flags by question (severe deep pain, photophobia, haloes, persistent visual loss, contact-lens use, hyperpurulent discharge)
Advise symptomatic self-care only — lubricant/artificial-tear drops and cool compresses — and explain antibiotics are not needed for a viral cause
Give strict infection-control / contagion advice (hand hygiene, single-use tissues, no shared towels/pillowcases, contact-lens avoidance until fully recovered)
Provide clear red-flag safety-netting and GP review if not improving in 3-5 days or if discharge becomes purulent
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to a pharmacist for over-the-counter lubricant drops and self-care advice is acceptable for this low-acuity presentation.
Advising simple lid hygiene with cooled boiled water in place of, or in addition to, cool compresses is acceptable.
A watchful-waiting plan with no medication at all, plus hygiene advice and safety-netting, is acceptable since the condition is self-limiting.
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen the sight-threatening red-eye red flags before reassuring
Failing to give infection-control / contagion advice for a highly contagious infection
Failing to provide specific red-flag safety-netting
errors_of_commission: Prescribing topical antibiotics for a clearly viral conjunctivitis
Recommending a topical corticosteroid without slit-lamp exclusion of HSV keratitis
Over-triaging a classic red-flag-negative red eye to ED or urgent ophthalmology for slit-lamp/fluorescein
Fabricating or asserting a slit-lamp finding, fluorescein result, measured visual acuity or preauricular-node exam the telehealth AI cannot obtain
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising a benign viral conjunctivitis AFTER a red-flag screen and giving symptomatic self-care plus contagion control and safety-netting. Prescribing antibiotics/steroids, or over-escalating a classic red-flag-negative red eye to ED/urgent ophthalmology, are the key failures. Self-care with hygiene advice and safety-netting is the correct, passing answer — this is a low-tier telehealth/pharmacist-manageable complaint.
